title: Georgia

jokes.text: Selina mispronouncing 'Madloba' repeatedly while the Georgian host corrects her
jokes.setup: Selina is giving a formal thank-you speech representing the United States at a Georgian election ceremony
jokes.punchline: She can't pronounce the Georgian word for 'thank you' and trails off into gibberish 'mad-lab-blah' while being corrected twice
jokes.explanation: Classic Selina incompetence on a world stage — she's supposed to be lending presidential stature but can't manage a single foreign word. The trail-off into gibberish is very her. Character-specific and economical.

jokes.text: Jesus, democracy. What a horror show this is.
jokes.setup: Selina has just given a speech about democracy being a cornerstone of her foundation
jokes.punchline: Immediately mutters 'Jesus, democracy. What a horror show this is.' — revealing her complete cynicism about the mission she just publicly championed
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy and character integration. The gap between the public speech and the immediate private reaction is perfectly timed, and 'What a horror show this is' is quintessential Selina — the woman who built a career on democracy while finding it tedious. Highly quotable and the contrast between solemn ceremony and her immediate dismissal is structurally impeccable.

jokes.text: I didn't even know Georgia was a country.
jokes.setup: Selina is on an official election-monitoring trip to the Republic of Georgia as a former US president
jokes.punchline: She admits she didn't know Georgia was a country — the former leader of the free world is geographically ignorant about the mission she just gave a speech on
jokes.explanation: A sharp joke about American political ignorance that is perfectly character-specific — Selina is exactly the kind of politician who would champion democracy in a country she knows nothing about. Well-delivered in context.

jokes.text: Do you have any water that doesn't come from a nuclear power plant, by any chance? / Vodka. / Okay, sure, fine. Vodka.
jokes.setup: Selina asks their Georgian guide for water, implying distrust of local water quality
jokes.punchline: The guide offers vodka as the only alternative; Selina accepts with resigned pragmatism ('Okay, sure, fine.')
jokes.explanation: The punchline is perfectly deadpan — the guide has no patience for diplomatic niceties. Selina's 'Okay, sure, fine' is funny in its resigned acceptance. Good economy, solid structure.

jokes.text: Hello! One day, you can grow up to be president. No, not you. Your brother.
jokes.setup: A Georgian man wants to introduce his daughter to the former US president and says she could grow up to be president
jokes.punchline: He immediately corrects himself — not the daughter, the brother. A casual patriarchal dismissal played as a throwaway
jokes.explanation: The joke lands on the dark irony that a woman who was president is being told this by a man who doesn't think women should be president. The beat timing — 'No, not you. Your brother.' — is crisp. Would hit harder for Selina's character specifically but the joke is on the Georgian man.

jokes.text: Selina Meyer travels the globe, spreading democracy like patient zero, right, Petradze? / Yes?
jokes.setup: Selina is enthusiastically pitching her 'second act' global democracy narrative to their guide
jokes.punchline: The 'patient zero' simile — democracy as a contagion she spreads — is darkly self-aware; Petradze's confused 'Yes?' deflates it further
jokes.explanation: The 'patient zero' metaphor is inspired — it frames American democracy-spreading as a disease outbreak. Petradze's monosyllabic non-comprehension is the perfect straight-man deflation. Good character integration: only Selina would be this obliviously self-congratulatory.

jokes.text: This place sucks my ghost nard.
jokes.setup: Selina is complaining about being in Georgia
jokes.punchline: The invented profanity 'ghost nard' — a Veep-ism that sounds obscene but is gloriously nonsensical
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep invented vulgarity. 'Ghost nard' is weird enough to land as funny through sheer absurdity. Highly quotable but structurally lightweight — it's just a colorful expression.

jokes.text: Why couldn't you have gotten me on an international election watching trip to Hawaii? / Hawaii is rightfully a monarchy and will be again.
jokes.setup: Selina complains about being sent to Georgia instead of somewhere nicer
jokes.punchline: The response that Hawaii 'is rightfully a monarchy and will be again' — a bizarre non-sequitur delivered with total confidence
jokes.explanation: The response is genuinely surprising and weird — it's not a joke about Hawaii being a state, it's a confident political stance nobody asked for. The confidence of 'and will be again' elevates it from throwaway to memorable. Great economy and originality.

jokes.text: Did you read the CODEL schedule of official events or were there not enough pictures for you?
jokes.setup: Selina is complaining about the schedule
jokes.punchline: Staff member insults Selina's intelligence by implying she needs pictures to read a document
jokes.explanation: A sharp staff-on-Selina insult that plays well in Veep's context where underlings frequently skewer their boss. The 'not enough pictures' line is specific and cutting.

jokes.text: Ooh, sex trafficking workshop. / It's an anti-sex trafficking workshop. / Lame.
jokes.setup: Selina is reading the official schedule of events
jokes.punchline: She misreads 'sex trafficking workshop' as something exciting, is corrected that it's anti-sex trafficking, and responds 'Lame' — finding the humanitarian purpose disappointing
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The three-beat structure is perfect: misread, correction, dismissal. 'Lame' as a response to the word 'anti' is shockingly funny because of its economy and moral vacancy. Only Selina Meyer would be disappointed that a sex trafficking workshop isn't actually about sex trafficking. High quotability, exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: It's gotta be 8:00 AM somewhere.
jokes.setup: Selina announces she needs a drink
jokes.punchline: Inverts the classic 'It's 5 o'clock somewhere' to suggest it's morning wherever she is, implying she'd drink at 8 AM
jokes.explanation: A clever inversion of a familiar expression — instead of excusing evening drinking with a later timezone, she excuses morning drinking with an earlier one. Smartly constructed but not groundbreaking.

jokes.text: Look at us, just like the good, old days except shittier in every conceivable way.
jokes.setup: The old team reunites in the hotel lobby
jokes.punchline: Selina greets her old staff with faux-nostalgia immediately undercut by 'except shittier in every conceivable way'
jokes.explanation: The structure of building sentiment ('just like the good old days') then destroying it ('except shittier in every conceivable way') is well-executed. 'Every conceivable way' has Veep's characteristic maximalist insult energy.

jokes.text: Maybe we can win an election for a change. / That came out wrong. / Yeah, way wrong, right?
jokes.setup: Mike is trying to be upbeat about the Georgia election-monitoring mission
jokes.punchline: He accidentally implies Selina's team lost elections, realizes it immediately, and the group awkwardly confirms how bad it was
jokes.explanation: Classic Mike McLintock foot-in-mouth moment. The callback to Selina's failed political career is well-timed and the group's piling on ('way wrong, right?') adds a beat. Character-specific but not especially original.

jokes.text: So he's got a goat to scape if things go wrong.
jokes.setup: Someone suggests Doyle picked Selina for the mission because of her elder statesman status
jokes.punchline: 'Goat to scape' — an intentional mangling of 'scapegoat' that correctly identifies the political logic while being linguistically absurd
jokes.explanation: The reverse-engineering of 'scapegoat' into 'goat to scape' is a clever piece of wordplay that also carries real political meaning. The joke earns its pun by being substantively correct about Selina's political utility.

jokes.text: Night and fog. Nacht und Nebel. / Yeah, I haven't missed that.
jokes.setup: Kent characterizes the political situation using the Nazi extermination program 'Nacht und Nebel' (Night and Fog) as metaphor, then translates it into German with complete seriousness
jokes.punchline: Someone responds 'Yeah, I haven't missed that' — treating Kent's characteristically dark, over-serious reference as just another Kent-ism
jokes.explanation: This is pure Kent Davidson — his tendency to reach for the most extreme historical reference possible, then translate it into German for no reason, is beautifully character-specific. The deflating response treats his darkness as utterly routine.

jokes.text: Okay, no. I don't get put on hold. Okay? When he comes on, he gets put on hold.
jokes.setup: Selina is told to hold for Secretary of State Doyle
jokes.punchline: She asserts a power hierarchy around hold music — as if this is the crucial remaining status battle of her post-presidency
jokes.explanation: The hold-music power struggle is a perfect encapsulation of Selina's status obsession in the post-presidency. Her insistence on never being on hold while the other person waits is petty and specific and absolutely her.

jokes.text: I just wanted to thank you for all that you've done for me. And I wanna let you know that I will destroy you in ways that are so creative, they will honor me for it at the Kennedy Center.
jokes.setup: Selina encounters Congressman Ryan, who betrayed her during a crucial House vote
jokes.punchline: She pivots from a faux-gracious 'thank you' into an elaborately creative threat that climaxes in the absurd image of the Kennedy Center honoring her for creative destruction
jokes.explanation: The Kennedy Center threat is exceptional. Starting with a gracious 'thank you' and pivoting to promised creative destruction is classic Selina misdirection. The specificity of the Kennedy Center — a prestigious arts honor — as the venue for recognizing her revenge is absurd, inspired, and perfectly Selina. One of the episode's most quotable moments.

jokes.text: Please hold for President Meyer. / Oh, you're putting me on hold. Actually, do you think you could not? / I'm here, I'm here... / Please hold for Secretary Doyle. / God damn it.
jokes.setup: Selina just demanded she never be put on hold — Ryan gets put on hold, then Selina is immediately put on hold herself
jokes.punchline: The exact situation she prohibited has happened to her within seconds; the 'God damn it' is the payoff
jokes.explanation: A tight callback that pays off the hold-music bit with perfect irony — Selina immediately suffers the fate she just prohibited. The chaos of multiple people being put on hold simultaneously escalates well. Rewatch value because you catch the setup-payoff more clearly.

jokes.text: I am gonna find ways to destroy you so hard that everybody at the Kennedy Center is gonna take a fucking massive shit.
jokes.setup: Selina is now talking to Andrew/Congressman Ryan directly and revisiting her threat
jokes.punchline: She tries to repeat the Kennedy Center threat but the wording degrades — now the Kennedy Center audience will 'take a fucking massive shit' — losing the wit of the original while gaining vulgarity
jokes.explanation: The callback to the Kennedy Center threat is intentional but this version is weaker — it trades the original's elegant absurdity for pure profanity. The joke is that Selina's repeat attempt is less polished, which is itself somewhat funny, but it's a diminishing return.

jokes.text: So I assume you're gonna beg for your job back. / It's a very kind offer, but I already have a better job working for President Meyer. I thought you knew that.
jokes.setup: Selina condescends to Andrew, assuming he's in a bad position
jokes.punchline: Andrew flips the power dynamic — he's already working for her and her assumption is wrong, delivered with 'I thought you knew that' as a polite extra twist of the knife
jokes.explanation: The reversal of the power dynamic is sharp — Selina tries to put Andrew in his place and he cheerfully deflates her. 'I thought you knew that' adds a condescending bonus beat.

jokes.text: I already have 434 brand-new best friends and we're all going to dinner tonight. And you're not invited. Sorry. Congressmen only.
jokes.setup: Selina is embarrassed by Andrew's reveal and tries to save face
jokes.punchline: She retreats to the pettiest possible response — excluding him from dinner like a middle schooler — with the formal 'Sorry. Congressmen only' delivered as though it's official policy
jokes.explanation: Selina's childish social exclusion — 'you're not invited' — played with the formality of a head of state is pitch-perfect character comedy. '434 brand-new best friends' reveals the desperate social arithmetic of a politician who lost everything.

jokes.text: I got a Georgian SIM card. Got text forwarding. I'll hear from you? All right, later. / I was suffering from dehydration and exhaustion. That's not the Buddy Calhoun I see in the mirror.
jokes.setup: Buddy Calhoun eagerly tries to join the congressmen going out, hinting about his contact details
jokes.punchline: Cut to a talking head where Buddy claims he stayed in due to 'dehydration and exhaustion' — obviously he was snubbed and is saving face; the self-pep talk 'That's not the Buddy Calhoun I see in the mirror' is the cringe kicker
jokes.explanation: The gap between the pathetic scene (him desperately hinting for an invitation) and the confessional excuse is a mockumentary classic. 'That's not the Buddy Calhoun I see in the mirror' is wonderfully absurd self-affirmation. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Although, it would be if you stepped out of the shower.
jokes.setup: Danny the TV host is interviewing Amy about Buddy's scandal; he says he doesn't see the Buddy Calhoun from the mirror
jokes.punchline: He adds 'Although, it would be if you stepped out of the shower' — a lascivious remark implying he's seen Buddy naked/exposed, referencing the scandal
jokes.explanation: A reasonably sharp line about the exposure scandal with a double meaning. The host's gleeful inappropriateness is the joke. Functional but not particularly original.

jokes.text: Can we roll that video one more time for our audience? / And here's the moment where he exposes himself. / That's right, yes. / Can we play that again?
jokes.setup: TV host Danny has video of Buddy Calhoun exposing himself to a policewoman
jokes.punchline: Danny keeps replaying the video mid-interview while Amy tries to maintain composure and speak professionally — the escalating repetition is the joke
jokes.explanation: The running gag of the video being replayed while Amy tries to speak is good cringe escalation. The host's professional framing ('for our audience') against what is clearly prurient interest works well.

jokes.text: In the interest of full disclosure, Ms. Brookheimer and I did have a brief relationship when she was a much younger woman.
jokes.setup: The TV interview is already deeply inappropriate when Danny randomly reveals
jokes.punchline: He and Amy had a relationship when she was 'a much younger woman' — a casually dropped bombshell that recontextualizes the entire uncomfortable interview
jokes.explanation: Perfectly timed revelation that makes everything retroactively more horrifying. 'When she was a much younger woman' is doing a lot of dark work. The interviewer is replaying her current fiancé's exposure video while they used to date — layers of cringe stacked high.

jokes.text: Ames, who's your favorite character on 'Downton'? / I don't know. Abby, I guess. / Yep.
jokes.setup: After the bombshell revelation that Danny and Amy dated, the segment ends and they're off-air
jokes.punchline: Danny makes mundane small talk about Downton Abbey; Amy gives a non-answer ('Abby, I guess' — there's no character named Abby); Danny's 'Yep' ends the scene in total awkward deflation
jokes.explanation: The silence IS the joke. After all the cringe, Danny's mundane pivot to Downton Abbey chat, Amy's confused non-answer about a character who doesn't exist, and Danny's 'Yep' closing the scene is masterful awkward comedy. The absurdity of 'Abby, I guess' being accepted without question adds a surreal layer.

jokes.text: He liked to insert in the anus of his opponents. / Yeah, and he did a beautiful rendition of 'Rendition.'
jokes.setup: Someone describes Murman as a brutal leader who 'ruled with an iron fist he liked to insert in the anus of his opponents'
jokes.punchline: Selina responds with a pun on 'rendition' — both a musical performance and the CIA's extrajudicial prisoner transfer program — delivered with cheerful lightness
jokes.explanation: A genuinely brilliant pun. The setup describes torture and abuse; Selina's 'beautiful rendition of Rendition' collapses musical performance and extrajudicial torture into one word with no apparent awareness of the horror. The casualness of her delivery ('he did a beautiful rendition') makes it land harder. One of the episode's sharpest wordplay moments.

jokes.text: You have a doctrine now? What is it, 'Boners are rare, don't waste them'?
jokes.setup: Someone mentions the 'Doyle Doctrine' — Secretary Doyle's foreign policy framework
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses Doyle's gravitas by suggesting his 'doctrine' is just advice about erections
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Boners are rare, don't waste them' as a foreign policy doctrine is inspired. It's cutting, crude, and perfectly encapsulates Selina's contempt for Doyle. Highly quotable Veep-style insult.

jokes.text: Hey, did you think of that while you were walking on the beach with a metal detector or however you fill your empty days?
jokes.setup: Selina continues attacking Doyle's intellect/relevance
jokes.punchline: She imagines him doing the most pathetic retiree activity (metal-detecting on a beach) as a stand-in for all post-political irrelevance
jokes.explanation: The metal detector image is wonderfully specific and degrading — it's the activity of someone with nothing left to contribute. 'Or however you fill your empty days' is a nice kicker that shows Selina's own anxieties about irrelevance.

jokes.text: Oh, fuck. Minna!
jokes.setup: Selina is told to take her lead from the UN Special Envoy, who turns out to be Minna Häkkinen
jokes.punchline: Selina's visceral 'Oh, fuck. Minna!' — the name itself functioning as a punchline to her dread
jokes.explanation: The economy here is excellent — the joke is entirely in the reaction. For fans of the show, Minna's arrival is dreaded by Selina and us simultaneously. The name as the punchline works perfectly.

jokes.text: You look absolutely radiant. / Oh, thank you. / Yes, your stay in the insane asylum, it's really agreed with you. / It was a spa. / No, no, a spa is where you go to get a massage and the like. You were in an insane asylum.
jokes.setup: Minna compliments Selina's appearance, and Selina thanks her
jokes.punchline: Minna reveals the compliment was a backdoor insult — Selina looked good because she was in an 'insane asylum,' not a spa. When Selina corrects her, Minna doubles down with clinical precision.
jokes.explanation: Minna at her best — the faux compliment that is actually devastating, delivered with the clinical certainty of someone who has never understood social norms. 'No, no, a spa is where you go to get a massage and the like. You were in an insane asylum.' has perfect comic rhythm and is pure Minna character integration. One of the episode's best insults.

jokes.text: I have such a big day tomorrow because I have an election to supervise. / I know, because I am supervising your supervising.
jokes.setup: Minna tries to exit the conversation by mentioning she has work to do
jokes.punchline: Selina reveals she's actually above Minna in the supervision chain, deflating Minna's attempt to exit with authority
jokes.explanation: 'I am supervising your supervising' is a nicely recursive power-play line. The Minna-Selina hierarchy reversal is satisfying and the brevity is good.

jokes.text: Your bidet is splashy and there's no terry cloth robe or shower cap. / Wait, you tested my bidet?
jokes.setup: Gary reports on problems with Selina's hotel room
jokes.punchline: He has personally tested her bidet — the implication of what that entails, combined with Selina's horrified realization, is the joke
jokes.explanation: Gary's servility crossing into the genuinely disturbing — he tested her bidet. 'Splashy' is a great specific detail. Selina's 'Wait, you tested my bidet?' lands perfectly as a horror-tinged realization. Pure Gary Walsh character comedy at its best.

jokes.text: Don't worry, I'm gonna fix this before tub time.
jokes.setup: Gary is reassuring Selina about her bathroom situation
jokes.punchline: 'Before tub time' — Gary refers to Selina's bath as 'tub time,' the kind of infantilizing language a parent uses for a child, revealing the nature of their codependent relationship
jokes.explanation: 'Tub time' is a tiny, perfect joke. It's only two words but it encapsulates the entire Gary-Selina dynamic: infantilizing servitude masquerading as care. Maximum economy, maximum character integration. A quintessential Gary Walsh moment.

jokes.text: Nobody speaks English here, everything was really confusing to me, and I was chased by children. And I think I may have voted.
jokes.setup: Mike is explaining why he needs Kent's help — he got lost trying to find a hotel room
jokes.punchline: The escalating list of disasters culminates in 'I think I may have voted' — the thing they're there to monitor has been accidentally corrupted by their own press secretary
jokes.explanation: The escalation from mundane problems (no English, confusing) to absurd (chased by children) to the jaw-dropping kicker (accidentally voted) is structurally excellent. 'I think I may have voted' is a perfectly understated bombshell for someone on an election-monitoring mission. One of the episode's standout moments.

jokes.text: Mike, that is the kind of voting irregularity that we are here to prevent.
jokes.setup: Mike explains he accidentally voted
jokes.punchline: Kent responds with complete bureaucratic seriousness — calling it 'the kind of voting irregularity we are here to prevent' — as if it were any other data point rather than their own guy doing it
jokes.explanation: Kent's response is pure character gold — he doesn't emote, he categorizes. The clinical application of election-monitoring language to their own staffer's accidental vote is peak Kent Davidson. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: That sounds bad. Look, please, can you help me get it off? / Unless you want the entire thumb removed with a utility blade, I suggest you find another person.
jokes.setup: Mike has an indelible ink mark on his thumb from voting and wants Kent's help
jokes.punchline: Kent's solution is clinical and extreme — thumb removal with a utility blade — delivered without any warmth or recognition that this is a disproportionate response
jokes.explanation: Kent offering thumb amputation as a practical solution is very on-brand. The specificity of 'utility blade' adds to the absurdity. Good Kent character integration.

jokes.text: Mom, I told you I get more homesick when you call.
jokes.setup: A snippet of Richard's phone call overheard in passing
jokes.punchline: Richard is telling his mom he gets MORE homesick when she calls — the opposite of the expected comfort from a parent call — delivered as a simple observation
jokes.explanation: Seven words that are perfectly Richard Splett — earnest, self-aware in the most literal way, and somehow both sweet and devastating. 'I get more homesick when you call' inverts the expected without any irony from Richard. Maximum economy, very high character integration.

jokes.text: Hi, what do you have that's like SpaghettiOs?
jokes.setup: The team is trying to find food in Georgia
jokes.punchline: Mike's food order at what is presumably a restaurant or hotel in the Republic of Georgia is 'something like SpaghettiOs' — the ultimate American tourist culinary regression
jokes.explanation: Mike asking for SpaghettiOs abroad is embarrassing in a very Mike McLintock way — the oblivious American abroad trope executed with specific brand accuracy.

jokes.text: I accidentally voted and now my thumb is green. / The same thing happened to me! Oh, my God!
jokes.setup: Mike reveals his accidental voting problem to Gary
jokes.punchline: Gary has also accidentally voted — the problem doubles, and Gary's panicked 'Oh, my God!' mirrors the absurdity
jokes.explanation: The callback/escalation that both the bumbling press secretary AND the bag-carrier have independently corrupted the election they're there to monitor is excellent. Gary's panic matches Mike's and together they become a comedic pair of election corruptors.

jokes.text: I went out to get a terry cloth robe and there was this crowd and I asked them where Nordstrom's was and then the next thing I know, they dyed my thumb and an old lady who smelled like cumin wouldn't stop hugging me!
jokes.setup: Gary explains how he accidentally voted
jokes.punchline: The story escalates from reasonable (looking for a robe) to surreal (asking for Nordstrom's in Tbilisi) to absurdist (old lady smelling of cumin hugging him) — all building to the same absurd conclusion as Mike
jokes.explanation: The parallel construction — both Gary and Mike accidentally voted — with Gary's version being more surreal and detailed (Nordstrom's in Georgia, cumin-smelling old lady) is well done. Asking for Nordstrom's in Tbilisi is particularly good.

jokes.text: Mine smelled like paprika.
jokes.setup: Gary describes an old lady who smelled like cumin who hugged him while he accidentally voted
jokes.punchline: Mike's addendum: his old lady 'smelled like paprika' — a hilariously specific comparative detail that treats the spice-scent of an Eastern European grandmother as the key data point
jokes.explanation: Three words, maximum impact. The parallel construction of Gary saying 'cumin' and Mike capping it with 'paprika' — with no other comment — is a masterclass in comedic economy. The specificity is what makes it: not just 'smelled like spices' but the careful differentiation of cumin vs. paprika as if this is important data. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: I can't afford to lose this job, Gary, but I can't afford to keep it either. I mean, it's so expensive to commute from DC. And I haven't been paid yet.
jokes.setup: Mike is panicking about the consequences of accidentally voting
jokes.punchline: His existential crisis resolves into the petty financial logistics of commuting — he's worried about parking costs, not the international election scandal
jokes.explanation: Mike's concerns scaling down from the macro (losing the job) to the micro (commuting costs, unpaid) is classic character diminishment. The fact that his primary concern is logistics rather than the ethical weight of corrupting an election is very Mike.

jokes.text: Okay, I never ever wanna see the inside of your mouth again. Okay? It looks like a Haitian porta-potty in there.
jokes.setup: Someone (Gary) has opened their mouth or made a noise near Selina at the food buffet
jokes.punchline: 'It looks like a Haitian porta-potty in there' — an extremely specific, unhinged insult about someone's oral hygiene/appearance
jokes.explanation: A Selina insult that is vivid but the geographic specificity feels somewhat cheap — the 'Haitian' modifier doesn't add wit so much as shock value. The 'porta-potty' image is effective but the punchline leans on a lazy ethnic punchdown that undermines the craft.

jokes.text: Just a spoonful of this caviar will pay for a wind turbine for a whole village. / Yeah, but do we really want these people to have electricity?
jokes.setup: Minna makes a humanitarian point about the obscene wealth on display at the reception
jokes.punchline: Selina's response genuinely questions whether the locals deserve electricity — a jaw-dropping expression of political cynicism dressed as a practical question
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Minna's humanitarian setup is the perfect foil for Selina's 'do we really want these people to have electricity?' — which is so casually awful it constitutes a perfect encapsulation of US foreign policy cynicism. The 'really' is doing enormous work, as is the word 'these.' Only Selina Meyer, former president of the United States, could say this to a UN envoy while eating caviar. Exceptional character integration and economy.

jokes.text: Have you imprisoned any good novelists recently?
jokes.setup: Selina warmly greets Murman, the authoritarian leader, at the reception
jokes.punchline: Her 'friendly' greeting includes a casual inquiry about novelist imprisonment — treating human rights abuses as small talk
jokes.explanation: Brilliant. 'Have you imprisoned any good novelists recently?' functions as a friendly icebreaker while being an indictment of Murman's authoritarianism. The 'good' qualifier — as if the quality of the novelists matters — is the perfect absurdist touch. Highly quotable, maximum economy, perfect character integration of Selina's cynical warmth.

jokes.text: I was sorry to see that your forceful condemnation did not do more to stop the recent genocide in the Congo. / Maybe next genocide. / Or the one after that.
jokes.setup: Murman takes a swipe at Minna's record as a UN envoy
jokes.punchline: Minna responds 'Maybe next genocide' — accepting the failure with cheerful optimism about future genocides; Selina piles on with 'Or the one after that'
jokes.explanation: Exceptional dark comedy. 'Maybe next genocide' is already remarkable; Selina's 'or the one after that' escalates it into a running gag about genocidal optimism. The UN envoy and the former US president cheerfully hoping for better performance at the next genocide is the show's political nihilism at peak form.

jokes.text: The only reason he hasn't been dragged naked through the streets and hung upside down from a lamp post with his cock stuffed in his mouth is because he took my advice from time to time. / Guilty as charged.
jokes.setup: Ben introduces himself as Murman's former advisor
jokes.punchline: Ben's extremely specific description of what would have happened to Murman without his advice (naked, lamppost, cock in mouth) — and Murman's cheerful 'Guilty as charged' in response to being a client, not the description
jokes.explanation: Ben's casually graphic description of Qaddafi-style fate is specifically designed to be shocking in its detail and casualness. 'Cock stuffed in his mouth' isn't gratuitous — it has historical referent to the fate of dictators. 'Guilty as charged' from Murman is the perfect non-reactive punchline.

jokes.text: Look, I saw your last election. No, thank you.
jokes.setup: Murman is told the US wants to make Georgia more like America (democratic elections)
jokes.punchline: He declines with 'I saw your last election. No, thank you.' — a devastating single-line critique of American democracy delivered by a literal dictator
jokes.explanation: Close to the episode's best joke. The irony is perfect: an authoritarian dictator declining American democracy as a model because he watched the US's own elections with dismay. The economy is extraordinary — six words do enormous satirical work. 'No, thank you' has the polite finality of a man who has genuinely considered and rejected the product. The joke works on multiple levels: critique of US elections, Murman's self-awareness, Selina's discomfort at the truth.

jokes.text: I cannot possibly accept that. [beat] I mean, even the appearance of a quid pro quo...
jokes.setup: Murman offers Selina a $10 million library donation
jokes.punchline: Selina's immediate refusal ('I cannot possibly accept that') followed by a long pause and the weasel-word 'I mean, even the APPEARANCE of a quid pro quo' — she's already finding her way toward accepting
jokes.explanation: The pause between 'I cannot possibly accept that' and the rationalization is doing heavy comedic lifting. Selina's process of talking herself toward corruption in real time — concern about 'appearance' rather than substance — is perfect character comedy and biting political satire simultaneously.

jokes.text: I trust I have made myself suitably clear. / As clear as the coffee table Danny Thomas had his hookers shit on.
jokes.setup: Murman wraps up their private discussion with diplomatic language
jokes.punchline: Selina uses the Danny Thomas coprophilia rumor as a simile for clarity — an extremely obscure piece of Hollywood gossip deployed as a diplomatic expression
jokes.explanation: The Danny Thomas glass coffee table is a genuine piece of Hollywood lore (whether true or not) repurposed as a metaphor for transparency in a diplomatic setting. The specificity ('coffee table,' 'hookers,' the glass clarity implication) is what elevates this above a generic gross-out line. Selina immediately adding 'It's an American expression' and Murman's 'yin and the yang' are bonus beats.

jokes.text: Your mother just added democracy to the foundation. So, now it's the Selina Meyer Foundation for Adult Literacy, Aids... / And the Advancement of Global Democracy.
jokes.setup: Catherine reports Selina has added democracy to the foundation's mission
jokes.punchline: The mission statement is comically bloated and the 'AIDS' acronym hangs awkwardly in the air before the full expansion is added — also the irony that Selina is accepting a bribe while adding democracy to her foundation
jokes.explanation: The joke of the bloated, performative foundation mission is fine but not sharp. The irony of adding 'democracy' while taking a bribe from a dictator is the real content here, which doesn't quite land in this line alone.

jokes.text: Marjorie and I have decided that we wanna have a baby, and we'd like to do it... / I will give you my sperm. / Oh, great!
jokes.setup: Catherine begins to explain a request to Dan — 'we'd like to do it...'
jokes.punchline: Dan doesn't wait for her to finish and immediately offers his sperm — the speed and the eagerness is the joke
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the Dan/Catherine section. Dan's instant, enthusiastic 'I will give you my sperm' — before she even finishes the sentence — is perfectly on-character for Dan Egan's gleeful narcissism and sexual availability. 'Oh, great!' from Catherine with no apparent surprise is an equally good beat. The structure is near-perfect: the interrupted setup, the premature punchline, the matter-of-fact acceptance.

jokes.text: Is there anything else that you wanna talk about? / I'm good. / Well, we'll send you the details. And if you could not ejaculate for the next 72 hours, that would be ideal.
jokes.setup: The sperm donation arrangement has been made with startling speed
jokes.punchline: Catherine's clinical 'if you could not ejaculate for the next 72 hours, that would be ideal' — treating this like a business arrangement while Dan is visibly already calculating
jokes.explanation: The clinical language ('that would be ideal') applied to ejaculation in a business context is good. Catherine's matter-of-fact delivery of reproductive logistics is very her.

jokes.text: Could we start the clock in, like, 30 minutes? / Yeah, I guess so. / Actually, no. Make it 40. The girl I'm thinking of likes to talk first. / Amber, it's Dan-Dan. You in midtown?
jokes.setup: Catherine says Dan shouldn't ejaculate for 72 hours, starting now
jokes.punchline: Dan negotiates a delay, then extends it ('make it 40'), reveals he already has a specific woman in mind, and immediately calls her — the joke escalates with each beat showing how instantly his mind went to sex
jokes.explanation: Dan Egan's character distilled into 30 seconds. The negotiation, the specific extension ('the girl I'm thinking of likes to talk first'), and the immediate call to 'Amber' in midtown — all while Catherine is still present — is perfectly escalating character comedy. 'Dan-Dan' as a self-nickname is an inspired detail.

jokes.text: That's keep a lot of docents in Rockports.
jokes.setup: Selina tells Ben about the $10 million library donation offer
jokes.punchline: Ben's response is to calculate the docent-in-Rockports value of the sum — treating it as a practical museum staffing question
jokes.explanation: 'Docents in Rockports' is a wonderfully specific image — the stereotype of museum volunteers in orthopedic shoes — used to measure bribe magnitude. Very Veep in its specificity.

jokes.text: I'd kill for a job where I could wear flats all day.
jokes.setup: Ben is contemplating Selina's ethical dilemma about the bribe
jokes.punchline: Ben's sudden, envious interjection about wanting to wear flat shoes — a completely personal non-sequitur in the middle of an ethics discussion
jokes.explanation: A perfect non-sequitur that reveals Ben's exhaustion with his life. Dropping a mundane complaint about footwear into an ethics discussion is great economy. Ben envying museum docents their comfortable shoes is a small gem.

jokes.text: I cannot lose my integrity. Without that, I am nothing. / How would we make sure no one finds out about the cash?
jokes.setup: Selina declares her integrity is her most important attribute
jokes.punchline: Ben immediately pivots to operational corruption planning — the gap between Selina's declaration and the next sentence is the joke
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best structural jokes. The whiplash from 'I cannot lose my integrity. Without that, I am nothing' to Ben's immediately practical 'How would we make sure no one finds out about the cash?' — with no pause, no acknowledgment — perfectly encapsulates how political ethics work. The structure is flawless.

jokes.text: What about Georgian law? / There is literally no Georgian law. And I'm using 'literally' correctly.
jokes.setup: They're checking whether the donation would be legal
jokes.punchline: 'There is literally no Georgian law. And I'm using literally correctly.' — a joke that is both a legal assessment AND a meta-commentary on common misuse of 'literally'
jokes.explanation: A brilliant double-layer joke: the content (Georgia has no functioning rule of law) is delivered through a meta-linguistic observation about the correct use of 'literally.' Pointing out that he's using it correctly implies he's aware most people don't — and the situation is genuinely so extreme it justifies the pedantry. Highly quotable and very smart.

jokes.text: See, at least when I was leader of the free world, people told me what to do.
jokes.setup: Selina is frustrated that Ben and Kent won't advise her on whether to take the bribe
jokes.punchline: She reveals that being 'leader of the free world' meant being told what to do — a perfect self-indictment of her presidency as puppet leadership
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. The irony of the 'leader of the free world' being the most directed person in the room is both a character revelation and a political statement about how executive power actually works. 'People told me what to do' as a nostalgic memory is deeply funny and deeply sad simultaneously.

jokes.text: I was thinking, Minna, would it be that bad if Murman actually did win? / Well, that depends how you define bad.
jokes.setup: Selina is now advocating for Murman winning the election she was sent to monitor for fairness
jokes.punchline: Minna's 'Well, that depends how you define bad' is a philosophically devastating response — it doesn't dismiss the question, it opens an abyss under it
jokes.explanation: Minna's response is quietly brilliant — 'that depends how you define bad' is both truthful (about geopolitical relativism) and a damning ethical statement. Good structural interplay between Selina's corrupt pragmatism and Minna's precision.

jokes.text: I think Murman is really good people. Honestly. And he's a hell of a storyteller. Really. You know, you could actually learn something from him, Minna.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to convince Minna that Murman, a torturer and poisoner, is fine actually
jokes.punchline: She praises him as 'good people' and 'a hell of a storyteller' — settling on narrative skills as a character reference for a war criminal
jokes.explanation: Selina reducing war criminal endorsement to 'he tells great stories' and suggesting Minna could 'learn something' from him is deliciously cynical. Minna as the audience for this pitch makes it funnier.

jokes.text: He has the soul of a poet. / Yeah, that and a car with a sunroof could've bought you my virginity in '83.
jokes.setup: Minna romantically defends Nikolai as having the soul of a poet
jokes.punchline: Selina's response implies her virginity was available for the price of a car with a sunroof in 1983 — a shockingly personal and crude non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'a car with a sunroof' as the price point, and '1983' as the year, gives this enormous comedic texture. Selina's self-deprecating sexual history delivered as a rejoinder to Minna's romanticism is peak Veep non-sequitur. Very quotable.

jokes.text: You were 22? / No, I was 15 in 1983. / Right. / Well, I was, Minna. / Right. / That's great.
jokes.setup: Minna calculates Selina's age from the 1983 reference and assumes she was 22
jokes.punchline: Selina reveals she was 15 — making the sunroof/virginity joke much darker — and Minna's repeated 'Right' and final 'That's great' are utterly inadequate responses to this revelation
jokes.explanation: The revelation that Selina was 15, not 22, recontextualizes joke 63 with a dark twist. Minna's inadequate 'Right... Right... That's great' as she processes this is exquisitely uncomfortable. The accumulation of Minna's increasingly hollow affirmations is brilliant structural comedy. 'That's great' as the final surrender is perfect.

jokes.text: Did I tell you that my son Otto tried to commit suicide and that he is now not speaking to me? / Did he throw himself from a moving town car?
jokes.setup: Minna shares that her son Otto attempted suicide and isn't speaking to her
jokes.punchline: Selina's first thought is the most specific, urban, Selina-world method of suicide — throwing oneself from a moving car in New York
jokes.explanation: Selina's complete failure of empathy is expressed through the weirdly specific 'town car' method — she's imagining it in terms of her world (Manhattan, hired cars). Her unsympathetic curiosity rather than condolence is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: No. No, he weighted his boots and he jumped into an ice hole. / I'm so sorry, into what? / Into an ice hole. Like a hole they cut in the ice... / Ah. / ...for fishing. / Right. / That makes sense.
jokes.setup: Minna explains her son jumped into an ice hole
jokes.punchline: Selina's confused, repeated requests for clarification and final 'That makes sense' — as if weighted boots and ice holes are something that 'makes sense' as a method — is the comedy of inappropriate response
jokes.explanation: 'That makes sense' after a suicide attempt description is wonderfully bleak. Selina's genuine confusion about ice-hole fishing vs. her earlier town-car assumption, and her relief when it's explained as fishing-related (somehow), is good cringe.

jokes.text: I wonder how much longer it is to get to the hotel. / I'm not really sure... / I think it's another 45, 50 minutes. / Oh, my God.
jokes.setup: In the middle of Minna discussing her son's suicide attempt, Selina abruptly changes the subject to logistics
jokes.punchline: The pivot from 'my son nearly died' to 'how much longer to the hotel' is jarring and Selina's 'Oh, my God' upon learning it's 45 minutes is more distress than she showed about Otto
jokes.explanation: The abrupt subject change mid-suicide-story is a masterpiece of Selina's self-absorption. Her 'Oh, my God' at the drive duration — more anguished than anything she said about Otto — is perfect character comedy. The structural contrast (serious topic/trivial pivot) is very well-executed.

jokes.text: Ooh, clink, clink. Hey, Pinocchios. Your noses must've been really tiny yesterday because you lied and now they're normal-sized.
jokes.setup: Buddy joins the congressmen who snubbed him the previous night
jokes.punchline: He accuses them of lying about 'hazing' him with the Pinocchio metaphor, but the math is wrong — if they lied, their noses would be bigger, not back to normal. The failed logic is itself the joke.
jokes.explanation: Buddy's Pinocchio metaphor is self-undermining in a way that's character-revealing — he's trying to seem clever but the logic doesn't track (lying makes noses grow, not shrink to 'tiny'). Whether intentional or not, it plays as Buddy being just slightly off.

jokes.text: By I, I mean the people of New Hampshire. In the immortal words of will.i.am, 'Tonight's gonna be really great!'
jokes.setup: Buddy says he's buying drinks to celebrate being accepted into the congressional group
jokes.punchline: He immediately clarifies it's the taxpayers paying; then quotes will.i.am as 'immortal words' with the self-consciously dorky formality of someone trying to seem cool
jokes.explanation: The 'people of New Hampshire' clarification is a good bit about political spending. 'In the immortal words of will.i.am' is trying hard but the paraphrase ('Tonight's gonna be really great!') is too generic to really land.

jokes.text: The furniture scene: 'Pick up your fucking chairs!' — Selina or aide screaming as a piece of furniture ends up somewhere absurd in the hotel.
jokes.setup: A chaotic background moment in the hotel — apparently involving misplaced or fallen furniture.
jokes.punchline: 'Pick up your fucking chairs!' — the outsize fury at a mundane furniture problem.
jokes.explanation: A quick physical comedy beat that's hard to fully assess without visuals. The mismatch of presidential outrage at furniture is mildly funny but doesn't have much depth from the transcript alone.

jokes.text: Fuck. / Are you fucking...?! / That's where that goes? / Pick up your fucking chairs!
jokes.setup: Selina and staff are getting into cars — visual comedy of people scrambling into vehicles and something going wrong with the seating arrangements
jokes.punchline: Selina's escalating profanity ('Fuck,' 'Are you fucking—?!', 'That's where that goes?', 'Pick up your fucking chairs!') suggests a physical farce with chairs/car doors going wrong
jokes.explanation: From the transcript alone this is a physical comedy sequence we can only partially assess. The escalating 'fucking' profanity pattern is a Veep staple and the absurd 'That's where that goes?' suggests genuine spatial chaos. Rewatch bonus for visual payoff.

jokes.text: Oh, she's kissing it. / Well, that's why we moisturize.
jokes.setup: Minna greets Nikolai by kissing his scarred/disfigured face from the poisoning
jokes.punchline: Selina's horrified aside 'She's kissing it' followed by someone's deadpan 'That's why we moisturize' — treating facial disfigurement as a general skincare reminder
jokes.explanation: 'That's why we moisturize' is a beautifully absurd non-sequitur — as if the lesson of Minna kissing Nikolai's acid-scarred face is about skincare maintenance. The logic is surreal but somehow makes perfect sense in the moment.

jokes.text: Oh, may I say that the reports of your beauty have not been exaggerated. / Well, back at you, red.
jokes.setup: Nikolai compliments Selina on her beauty with formal elegance
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'Well, back at you, red' — an extremely casual, slightly insulting response to a formal compliment, calling attention to his red scarring/disfigurement with a nickname
jokes.explanation: 'Back at you, red' is a stellar piece of Selina insensitivity — she responds to a formal diplomatic compliment with a casual deflection that also nicknames him after his visible scarring. The gap between Nikolai's eloquence and Selina's 'back at you, red' is enormous and very funny.

jokes.text: Ben about Nikolai: 'As long as the check clears.'
jokes.setup: Nikolai says Ben is 'doing God's work.'
jokes.punchline: Ben's response confirms he has no ideological commitment — only financial.
jokes.explanation: Classic Ben amoral cynicism, compressed to five words. Not original as a concept but extremely well-executed with maximum economy.

jokes.text: Who needs all that champagne when you can have all these different kinds of wallpaper?
jokes.setup: Nikolai contrasts his humble apartment with Murman's vulgar palace
jokes.punchline: Selina sarcastically praises his 'different kinds of wallpaper' as the equivalent of champagne — noting the eccentrically decorated apartment is not actually a good substitute
jokes.explanation: Good Selina deadpan — the wallpaper observation is specific enough to suggest she's actually looking at it and underwhelmed. The formulation 'different kinds of wallpaper' is funnier than just 'ugly wallpaper.'

jokes.text: Selina, you see right through me. / What? / It's because we are close as sisters. / No, we're not.
jokes.setup: Minna dramatically claims Selina sees through her
jokes.punchline: Selina's flat 'No, we're not' to the 'close as sisters' assertion — with no diplomatic cushioning — is perfectly blunt
jokes.explanation: Selina's refusal to play along with Minna's emotional framing is a reliable source of comedy. 'No, we're not' is blunter than expected and lands well.

jokes.text: Nikolai and I are not only bound by mutual respect and shared beliefs. We are also bound by powerful physical attraction. / Oof. / You gotta be kidding. / We are, in fact, lovers. / Lovers. / Lovers. / With Scab Calloway?
jokes.setup: Minna reveals that she and the heavily scarred/disfigured Nikolai are romantically involved
jokes.punchline: The group's escalating reactions ('Oof,' 'You gotta be kidding,' the repeated 'Lovers' like they can't process it) and Selina's nickname 'Scab Calloway' for Nikolai are the payoff
jokes.explanation: 'Scab Calloway' is one of the episode's best coinages — a reference to Cab Calloway recast through Nikolai's scarring. The group's repeated stunned 'Lovers' echoing each other is textbook cringe escalation. High impact, very quotable.

jokes.text: His very touch makes me quiver. / Okay, and now we get to eat food.
jokes.setup: Minna continues to describe her physical attraction to scarred Nikolai
jokes.punchline: Selina's immediate pivot to food — 'Okay, and now we get to eat food' — is her standard coping mechanism when confronted with something unbearable
jokes.explanation: 'Okay, and now we get to eat food' is perfect comedic shutdown — the 'and now' signals Selina is mentally filing 'Minna's sex life' under 'done' and moving on. 'We get to eat food' sounds like she's talking to a child about a treat. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Eat it. / What? / Eat the soup. / Why? / Because I'm hungry. / I need to know if it's been poisoned.
jokes.setup: Selina is sitting next to Nikolai at dinner; he has been poisoned before in a restaurant
jokes.punchline: He asks Selina to taste his soup to check for poison — using the former US president as a food taster — and his reason for asking is simply 'Because I'm hungry'
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured sequence. 'Eat it / What? / Eat the soup / Why? / Because I'm hungry' — the reason 'because I'm hungry' is so bathetically simple for something (testing for poison) so grave. Then the real reason comes and it's more alarming: he wants a human poison-tester. One of the episode's best constructed scenes.

jokes.text: It's delicious. / Okay. / Is there anything happening to me? / I don't think so. / Oh, it has carrots in it. I'm not gonna eat that.
jokes.setup: Selina has tasted Nikolai's soup as a poison test and reports back
jokes.punchline: After risking her life for a poison taste test, Nikolai decides not to eat the soup anyway — because it has carrots. The stakes collapse from assassination to vegetable preference.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke. The entire setup of poison fear, Selina's brave soup-tasting, the anxious 'Is there anything happening to me?' — all demolished by 'Oh, it has carrots in it. I'm not gonna eat that.' The escalation from mortal danger to vegetable aversion is structurally perfect. The joke works because the letdown is so complete and Nikolai's carrot aversion is treated with the same gravity as the poison concern. Near-flawless comedy construction.

jokes.text: I have been reliably told that war criminal Murman had the audacity to offer you a $10 million bribe. / Okay, well, that is ridiculous. / I agree. It is so much less than you are worth.
jokes.setup: Nikolai accuses Murman of offering Selina a $10 million bribe; Selina denies the bribe was real
jokes.punchline: Nikolai agrees it's 'ridiculous' — but for the opposite reason: $10 million is too little for her. The misdirection from 'bribe is wrong' to 'bribe is too small' is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Extraordinary setup/punchline/misdirection. Selina's 'that is ridiculous' is meant to signal moral indignation; Nikolai's agreement ('I agree') makes us think he's backing her up — then 'It is so much less than you are worth' reveals they were never on the same page. The structure is close to perfect: two characters appearing to agree on ethics who are actually agreeing on market pricing. One of the episode's standout moments.

jokes.text: How dare you, sir! I will remind you that I am the former president of the United States. You have lost a great deal of face in my estimation. / What I meant to say, of course, is I would like to make a $15 million donation to your library.
jokes.setup: Nikolai explicitly offers a '$15 million bribe'; Selina performs outrage
jokes.punchline: Nikolai immediately rephrases it as a 'donation' and Selina's outrage evaporates — it was all theater
jokes.explanation: The callback to the Murman donation negotiation, now with Nikolai, shows how the corruption ritual has been codified. Selina's theatrical outrage and instant reset is perfect character comedy — she knows the game and so does Nikolai.

jokes.text: Well, that is something we can discuss as long as we understand that there is, in fact, no understanding.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to accept the bribe while maintaining deniability
jokes.punchline: Her formulation 'we can discuss this as long as we understand there is no understanding' is a self-contradicting diplomatic euphemism for accepting a bribe
jokes.explanation: The recursive logic of 'no understanding' as the agreed understanding is almost Catch-22 level political doublespeak. It perfectly encapsulates how political corruption is laundered through language. High quotability and strong character integration — only Selina could say this with a straight face.

jokes.text: In reality, although I teach at the university and lead the opposition party, I control a telecommunication monopoly and own the most popular soccer team, the Tbilisi Bauxite Miners.
jokes.setup: Nikolai shares his 'secret' — that despite the humble apartment, he's actually very wealthy
jokes.punchline: The soccer team name 'Tbilisi Bauxite Miners' — a perfectly unglamorous, industrial name for a team owned by a secret oligarch posing as a reformer
jokes.explanation: 'Tbilisi Bauxite Miners' is a beautifully specific absurd detail — bauxite mining as a team identity is wonderfully unglamorous. The revelation that the democracy reformer is also a telecom monopolist and sports-team owner is good satire.

jokes.text: If you told him that rava ganavlis was chakapuli, he would eat it up and ask for seconds. / I can only hope that rava ganavlis is human shit. / It is actually what you vomit after eating shit.
jokes.setup: Nikolai makes a joke about Secretary Doyle being gullible using Georgian food terms
jokes.punchline: Selina hopes 'rava ganavlis' means 'human shit'; Nikolai's clarification that it's 'what you vomit after eating shit' is a spectacular escalation beyond what Selina was hoping for
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest jokes. Selina's hope ('I can only hope that rava ganavlis is human shit') is already funny — she's aspiring upward from the baseline. Then Nikolai's correction elevates it beyond the possible: it's what you vomit AFTER eating shit. The escalation beyond Selina's imagination, the deadpan delivery, and the diplomatic context make this exceptional. 'I like you, dimples' as the follow-up seals it.

jokes.text: I like you, dimples.
jokes.setup: After the rava ganavlis/vomit-after-eating-shit exchange
jokes.punchline: Nikolai's approving nickname for Selina — 'dimples' — delivered as a warm capstone to bonding over discussing vomit-after-eating-shit
jokes.explanation: Two words, maximum economy. The affectionate 'dimples' as a bond-sealing moment after a conversation about excrement is perfectly timed and oddly sweet. Nikolai naming her after her best physical feature right after their worst possible topic is a beautiful contrast.

jokes.text: Nikolai: 'I like you, dimples.'
jokes.setup: Nikolai and Selina have just bonded over a scatological joke.
jokes.punchline: Nikolai's verdict — 'I like you, dimples' — is the warmest possible reaction to one of the most revolting conversations in the episode.
jokes.explanation: Three words that work beautifully as a button. The nickname 'dimples' is an unexpected term of endearment from a scarred Georgian oligarch. It also humanizes Nikolai instantly in the strangest way. Economy is outstanding.

jokes.text: This is fucking beast! / It's very interesting. / Hey, put that on. All the cool guys wear the band's T-shirt. / You know, the band's name translates to Panzer Division. / What?
jokes.setup: Richard and Buddy are at a Georgian concert and Richard is enthusiastically buying band merch
jokes.punchline: After enthusiastically wearing the T-shirt, Richard learns the band is called 'Panzer Division' — a Nazi armored unit — making his tourist excitement immediately inappropriate
jokes.explanation: The oblivious American tourist wearing Nazi-themed band merch because 'all the cool guys wear the band's T-shirt' is good dark comedy. Richard's enthusiasm and Buddy's 'What?' are well-timed. 'This is fucking beast!' from Richard — clearly in very un-Richard territory — is a nice character note.

jokes.text: Ryan and the congressman slowly backing out of the Panzer Division concert: 'I think maybe we should leave.' / 'Uh-huh.' / 'Excuse us. Pardon me.'
jokes.setup: They've realized they're at a neo-fascist concert.
jokes.punchline: The polite 'excuse us, pardon me' while extricating themselves from a fascist rock show is perfectly British-American understatement.
jokes.explanation: The callback works. The politeness of 'excuse us, pardon me' in the context of exiting a fascist concert is good deadpan. Primarily a visual beat that works better on screen but holds up in transcript.

jokes.text: The poison? It did not engorge only his face, if you know what I mean. / I think I do. / It also engorged his penis and made it very unusual texture of sea cucumber, so it's great for vaginal orgasms.
jokes.setup: Minna is discussing what Selina thought of Nikolai; she begins with 'Can I be uncharacteristically blunt?'
jokes.punchline: Minna reveals the poison engorged not just Nikolai's face but also his penis to a 'sea cucumber' texture that is 'great for vaginal orgasms' — delivered with clinical Finnish matter-of-factness
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most memorable lines. 'Sea cucumber' as the texture descriptor, the clinical 'great for vaginal orgasms' assessment, and Minna's total lack of embarrassment in sharing this is peak Minna Häkkinen character comedy. The setup 'I think I do' followed by the explanation showing Selina had absolutely no idea is perfectly structured. Deeply quotable.

jokes.text: Minna to Selina: 'Can I be uncharacteristically blunt with you?' / Selina: 'I can't imagine what that...' — then Minna reveals Nikolai's poisoned genitals.
jokes.setup: Minna asks permission to be blunt — unusual for her — and Selina's 'I can't imagine what that...' implies Minna is never blunt.
jokes.punchline: The 'uncharacteristic' bluntness turns out to be a detailed description of Nikolai's poison-engorged, sea-cucumber-textured penis.
jokes.explanation: The 'uncharacteristically blunt' framing is the perfect setup for the most explicit thing Minna has said. Selina's incomplete 'I can't imagine what that...' is cut off and replaced by the reality, which is much more specific than anything she imagined. Good structure.

jokes.text: Which do you prefer, vaginal or clitoral orgasms? / You know, I just come and get it over with. / You can come just from your mind?
jokes.setup: Minna continues her clinical sexual interrogation of Selina
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'I just come and get it over with' — a line about efficient orgasm — is misread by Minna as 'coming from your mind alone' — a completely different interpretation that reveals Minna's literal-mindedness
jokes.explanation: The misunderstanding is brilliantly constructed — Selina's 'come and get it over with' is about efficiency; Minna interprets 'come' as mental orgasm. The gap between what was said and what was heard produces two very different images. 'You can come just from your mind?' is an incredible line. Minna's sincere belief that Selina has psychic orgasms is somehow both the funniest and most Minna possible response.

jokes.text: What can I do to get you to stop talking? / Now? / Well, you have to walk 25 miles for breast cancer and attend a WNBA game, but I think we're gonna be okay.
jokes.setup: Selina wants Minna to stop talking about orgasms
jokes.punchline: Minna takes 'what can I do?' literally and provides an actual task list: walking 25 miles for breast cancer and attending a WNBA game — the specificity of these two requirements is the joke
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Minna joke. Taking Selina's rhetorical 'what can I do to get you to stop?' as a literal request for tasks, then providing two extremely specific charitable/sporty prerequisites — 25 miles for breast cancer AND a WNBA game — is pure absurdist Minna. The word 'Now?' before the list is a perfect setup for a multi-part answer. 'But I think we're gonna be okay' as a reassurance at the end is the ideal capstone. One of Minna's best moments in the episode.

jokes.text: Amy, that morning when I heard you say that you were happiest curled up next to me eating popcorn and watching 'Downton Abbey' I figured it all out.
jokes.setup: Buddy is backstage at an event and has just seen Amy's TV interview
jokes.punchline: He genuinely believes Amy's fabricated 'happiest curled up with you' comment from the interview — the thing she said purely for cameras has now inspired Buddy to make a life decision
jokes.explanation: The callback to Amy's calculated interview comment, now being used by Buddy to justify a major life decision, is good structural payoff. The irony that her least sincere moment is what he believes is well-constructed.

jokes.text: Buddy Calhoun announces he's withdrawing from the governor's race because of 'the love of the wonderful woman who has agreed to be my wife' — Amy, who had no idea this was coming.
jokes.setup: Buddy has misread Amy's TV interview comment about being happiest with him on the couch as a declaration of love.
jokes.punchline: He announces his withdrawal from the race and calls Amy out to stand beside him in front of the cameras — she said it for the cameras and now has to stand by it for the cameras.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Amy's TV performance lands hard. She said what she said 'for the cameras' and now has to live the consequences in front of cameras. The irony is complete. Amy's horror at being summoned — 'Amy, darling, come on out here' — is a great cringe beat.

jokes.text: Amy, that morning when I heard you say that you were happiest curled up next to me eating popcorn and watching 'Downton Abbey' I figured it all out. The next governor of Nevada, Buddy Calhoun!
jokes.setup: Amy told Danny on TV that she was happiest with Buddy watching Downton Abbey — she later admitted she was just saying it for cameras
jokes.punchline: Buddy heard it, took it literally, and has based his entire political future on a PR performance — the irony that his heartfelt decision is founded on Amy's calculated lie
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed callback that pays off the Downton Abbey line from earlier. The irony — Amy's fabricated TV warmth accidentally triggers Buddy's genuine life decision — is the show doing structural comedy across an episode.

jokes.text: I need to get out of this toxic world of politics and start to appreciate the things that really do matter in life. / No, Buddy! / Like the love of the wonderful woman who has agreed to be my wife.
jokes.setup: Buddy announces he's withdrawing from the governor's race based on Amy's fabricated sentiment
jokes.punchline: The crowd's 'No!' as he withdraws — and Amy's presence being summoned — knowing it was all built on a lie Amy told on TV
jokes.explanation: The cringe of watching a political career end because of something his fiancée said purely for cameras is good Veep-level irony. 'Amy, darling, come on out here, honey' while Amy knows the truth is cringe-rich.

jokes.text: Murman's leading by more votes than there are people in the country. / Yeah, maybe he's bussing them in from Chicago.
jokes.setup: The election results show obvious fraud — more votes than people
jokes.punchline: Ben's quip about bussing them in from Chicago — a reference to the legendary Chicago political machine's ballot stuffing — treats Georgian election fraud as a familiar American phenomenon
jokes.explanation: A solid political joke that draws a line between Georgian ballot fraud and the American machine politics tradition. The casual Chicago reference implies the US is in no position to criticize. Good economy.

jokes.text: This election's going down like Eleanor Roosevelt at Dinah Shore Weekend.
jokes.setup: Selina is reacting to the obviously fraudulent election results
jokes.punchline: The Eleanor Roosevelt/Dinah Shore Weekend simile — implying Eleanor Roosevelt was a lesbian attendee at the famous gay resort weekend — delivered as a casual expression of electoral collapse
jokes.explanation: A very specific historical/cultural reference joke that rewards knowledgeable viewers. The image of Eleanor Roosevelt at Dinah Shore Weekend (a lesbian resort gathering) as a metaphor for something 'going down' has multiple registers. Selina's casual deployment of this obscure reference is funny in itself.

jokes.text: Well, do I work for you? Just answer it. / Okay. / Right?
jokes.setup: Selina's phone is ringing and Gary is nearby
jokes.punchline: Selina tells Gary to answer her phone as if this is unreasonable to even question; her 'Right?' after is seeking validation for her reasonable-to-her entitlement
jokes.explanation: A functional Selina-Gary entitlement beat. The 'Right?' seeking validation for demanding Gary answer her phone is good character comedy but not particularly fresh territory.

jokes.text: Well, do I work for you? Just answer it.
jokes.setup: Gary says Selina's phone is ringing
jokes.punchline: Selina asks if she works for Gary — implying Gary should answer her phone — the inversion of the employer/employee relationship
jokes.explanation: Competent Selina-Gary dynamic joke but not exceptional. The inversion is standard.

jokes.text: Please hold. It was an accident. / Mike voted, too, Mike voted, too. / What?! / I can't believe you! / Stop it. / You two ding-dongs look like you fingered the Incredible Hulk.
jokes.setup: Gary accidentally puts Murman on hold; then Mike's accidental vote is revealed to Selina during a crisis
jokes.punchline: 'You two ding-dongs look like you fingered the Incredible Hulk' — Selina's insult for the two green-thumbed accidental voters
jokes.explanation: 'You two ding-dongs look like you fingered the Incredible Hulk' is the episode's most quotable single line. It's a perfect callback resolution to the accidental voting subplot — 'ding-dongs' is the right level of contemptuous endearment, and 'fingered the Incredible Hulk' is an inspired image for green ink on fingers. Economy and imagery are both exceptional.

jokes.text: Madam President, let me tell you little story about growing up in the Gurjaani countryside. / You thought you caught a fish, it turned out to be a tire. The tire was full of fish. It's a great story. It's a very valuable lesson, but I really gotta go. / 20 million.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to turn down Murman's bribe; he begins one of his 'countryside stories' she was trying to avoid earlier
jokes.punchline: Selina preemptively narrates the entire fish/tire story herself (correctly), then still tries to leave — only for Murman to immediately say '20 million' instead of finishing the story
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly constructed callback. Selina has been subjected to enough Murman countryside stories that she can now speed-run them herself ('You thought you caught a fish, it turned out to be a tire. The tire was full of fish. It's a great story.'). Her summary and endorsement ('very valuable lesson') while trying to escape is perfect. Then '20 million' as Murman's counter replaces the story entirely — the story was always just leverage. One of the episode's best constructed moments.

jokes.text: Well, Murman, that is a big tire with a fuckload of fish in it.
jokes.setup: Murman has offered $20 million and is waiting for Selina's response
jokes.punchline: Selina uses Murman's own fish/tire metaphor to accept the larger bribe — adopting the corruption's language to participate in it
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect callback execution. Selina not only accepts the metaphor but extends it with 'a fuckload of fish' — the vulgarity crashing into the folksy imagery is inspired. She's speaking Murman's language now, which signals her complete capitulation to the corruption. The line is structurally beautiful, character-specific, and extremely quotable. One of the episode's top three jokes.

jokes.text: Murman: '20 million.' / Selina: 'Well, Murman, that is a big tire with a fuckload of fish in it.'
jokes.setup: Murman has escalated his bribe to $20 million.
jokes.punchline: Selina adopts his own fish/tire metaphor to acknowledge the offer — 'a big tire with a fuckload of fish in it.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Selina taking ownership of Murman's metaphor — extending it with 'a big tire with a fuckload of fish in it' — is perfect. It signals her acceptance while preserving the register of his storytelling. 'Fuckload' is the exact right word. The callback elevates both the original joke and this payoff. High marks across all dimensions.

jokes.text: How much? / 20 million. / Yahtzee. / I mean, they're both crooks. / What's the difference? / $5 million. / Exactly.
jokes.setup: The team is processing the competing bribe offers from Murman ($20M) and Nikolai ($15M)
jokes.punchline: 'Yahtzee.' The entire team's rapid-fire moral calculus: both are crooks, the difference is $5M, done.
jokes.explanation: 'Yahtzee' as the response to '$20 million bribe accepted' is a delightful word choice — triumphant, game-show, utterly inappropriate to the gravity of accepting a geopolitical bribe. The subsequent moral math ('they're both crooks / what's the difference / $5 million / exactly') compresses the entire ethical framework of the episode into five exchanges. Exceptional structure and economy.

jokes.text: Well, that is very strong language, Minna. / Yes. / Yes, and I wonder if we might phrase it a different way. / For instance, perhaps we would say there have been certain irregularities in this election. / Yes! / And that is not uncommon for a young democracy. / Yes. / And, therefore, we believe that Murman is the uncontested winner. / Now, are you aware that that is the exact opposite of what you said just a moment ago? / Yes. / But I've had time to reflect since that moment.
jokes.setup: Minna is prepared to condemn the election; Selina (now bribed) tries to talk her into endorsing it
jokes.punchline: Minna's 'Yes. But I've had time to reflect since that moment.' — acknowledging she's saying the opposite of what she said minutes ago with complete equanimity, attributing it to 'reflection'
jokes.explanation: The step-by-step reversal of Minna's position, each step confirmed with 'Yes,' leading to the concession that it's the exact opposite of her previous position, is well-constructed political satire. 'I've had time to reflect since that moment' — 'that moment' being about 60 seconds ago — is the perfectly absurd capstone.

jokes.text: My concern is I wonder if your judgment is being clouded by your feelings that are brought on by Nikolai's lumpy poison cock.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to explain why Minna changed her mind about the election
jokes.punchline: Selina's clinical and precise characterization: 'Nikolai's lumpy poison cock' — the source of Minna's cognitive impairment
jokes.explanation: An excellent callback that synthesizes the sea cucumber/poison penis description into a diplomatic accusation. 'Lumpy poison cock' as a clinical diagnosis for bad judgment is a great distillation. Selina deploying this as her argument while she herself has just accepted $20M in bribes is perfect irony.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. Well... I will recuse myself. Immediately, I will resign. / No, no, no, let's not overreact. Just take a deep breath, Minna. You're just in the middle of what we in America call... / A difficult situation. / ...a fuck fog.
jokes.setup: Selina has accused Minna of being in a 'fuck fog' (compromised by sex with Nikolai) and Minna is dramatically overreacting
jokes.punchline: Ben tries to supply the American term ('a difficult situation') but Selina overrides with 'a fuck fog' — then delivers it as if it's an established American colloquialism
jokes.explanation: 'A fuck fog' presented as an established American cultural concept — with Ben's straight-man 'a difficult situation' attempt — is a great three-way comedy structure. 'Fuck fog' is an instant coinage that's both crude and surprisingly apt. Selina's presentation of it as a known American term elevates it from vulgarity to commentary.

jokes.text: You're just in the middle of what we in America call... — A difficult situation. — ...a fuck fog.
jokes.setup: Selina begins to define the American term for Minna's situation
jokes.punchline: Someone intervenes with 'a difficult situation'; Selina corrects: 'a fuck fog'
jokes.explanation: 'Fuck fog' is a wonderful coinage — the state of impaired judgment caused by sexual obsession. The setup (someone tries to defuse with 'difficult situation') and Selina's correction makes it land harder. The Minna follow-up — 'In Finland we call this the fever of the sausage' — is the perfect callback that proves the universal concept. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Yeah, in Finland, we call this the fever of the sausage. / Okay, so, then you get it.
jokes.setup: Selina has just coined 'fuck fog' as an American expression
jokes.punchline: Minna provides the Finnish equivalent: 'the fever of the sausage' — perfectly capturing Finnish bluntness and sausage-culture in one surreal phrase
jokes.explanation: The episode's funniest single line. 'The fever of the sausage' as Finland's equivalent of 'fuck fog' is transcendent. It's simultaneously absurd (sausage-based metaphor for sexual obsession), culturally specific (Finnish love of sausage, Finnish linguistic directness), and structurally perfect as Minna's rejoinder. 'Okay, so, then you get it' from Selina treating this as a perfectly reasonable confirmation is the ideal capstone. This approaches the calibration anchor for 9-10 territory.

jokes.text: You won't tell anyone, Selina? / Thank you so much. You are such a good friend. / Oh, well... / Sometimes I don't feel worthy of this friendship. / Mm, well, sometimes I feel that way, too.
jokes.setup: Minna thanks Selina for keeping her secret about Nikolai
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'sometimes I feel that way, too' — agreeing that she herself is not worthy of the friendship — delivered as a sincere reciprocal sentiment that is actually a devastating accidental insult
jokes.explanation: The joke is that 'sometimes I feel that way, too' — meaning Selina also sometimes feels she doesn't deserve the friendship — is read as an insult about Minna. But it's actually Selina being sincere about her own inadequacy while accidentally confirming Minna's doubts. The moment plays in the gap between what was meant and what was heard. Structurally elegant.

jokes.text: I mean, honestly, that is the most grotesque country I have ever been to and I have been all over Florida.
jokes.setup: Selina is on the plane home from Georgia
jokes.punchline: She rates Georgia as more grotesque than anywhere she's been — then qualifies her travel experience as primarily Florida, the joke being that Florida is her baseline for comparison
jokes.explanation: The joke turns on 'I have been all over Florida' — Georgia (the country) is worse than everywhere in Florida, which is a damning reference point. The structure is excellent: establish the superlative, undercut with the benchmark. Florida as a stand-in for American grotesquerie is a precise and economical choice.

jokes.text: ...seized by military elements loyal to Oleg Petradze. / Wait a minute, is that our guide? / I think that's the guy I voted for.
jokes.setup: News reports that Murman has been arrested by military elements loyal to Oleg Petradze
jokes.punchline: Mike (or Gary) realizes Petradze is the guy they accidentally voted for — tying the accidental voting subplot to the coup resolution
jokes.explanation: The perfect callback resolution: the guide they've been dismissing all episode (Petradze) has staged a coup, and Mike/Gary accidentally voted for the guy who took over the country. 'I think that's the guy I voted for' is structurally the best callback payoff in the episode — their bumbling irrelevance accidentally contributed to the outcome. Exceptional.

jokes.text: Wow, that's like Gary becoming president. / From the tea party. / No. / Oh, yeah, that is funny.
jokes.setup: Someone compares Petradze's surprising coup to an unlikely scenario
jokes.punchline: The comparison is made to Gary becoming president; Gary is so insignificant and servile that even this is insulting to him — then the addition 'From the tea party' and Gary's dignified 'No' and the group's 'Oh, yeah, that is funny' piling on
jokes.explanation: Gary as the baseline for 'most unlikely president' is well-used character comedy. The 'From the tea party' addition and Gary's hurt 'No' then the group laughing at him specifically is a good pile-on beat.

jokes.text: 'Murman's gonna have a lot of great stories to tell the firing squad.'
jokes.setup: Murman has been arrested on live TV before being sworn in.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to an authoritarian's arrest is to note that he'll at least have entertaining execution stories — prioritizing narrative quality over the human rights situation.
jokes.explanation: A great callback to Murman's constant attempts to tell his Gurjaani countryside stories. The callback works perfectly — his defining character trait (excessive storytelling) will be of limited use at a firing squad. Dark, specific, and efficient. High quotability.

jokes.text: Yeah, that's worth about $20 million. / Well, actually, since the coup, there's been a slight dip in the exchange rate. It's now worth approximately $389,000. Way to go, ma'am.
jokes.setup: Selina's library received a $20 million donation from a Georgian foundation; the aide translates: 60 million Georgian lari
jokes.punchline: Richard cheerfully reports the coup has tanked the exchange rate — the $20 million is now worth $389,000. 'Way to go, ma'am.'
jokes.explanation: The perfect karmic payoff for the entire bribe arc. Selina spent the episode negotiating up from $10M to $20M, compromised her integrity, endorsed a fraudulent election — and the currency collapse has reduced her take to $389,000. 'Way to go, ma'am' from Richard, with his characteristic cheerful obliviousness, is the ideal punchline. Structurally, this is the episode's best constructed payoff beat.

jokes.text: The $20 million Georgian AIDS foundation donation — received the previous night — is now worth approximately $389,000 due to the coup-devalued exchange rate.
jokes.setup: Selina accepted a $20 million bribe. She compromised her entire mission for it.
jokes.punchline: Due to the coup she helped enable, the Georgian currency has crashed and the donation is now worth $389,000 — 'Way to go, ma'am.'
jokes.explanation: A structural triumph. The entire episode's bribery escalation — Murman's $10M, Nikolai's $15M, $20M, 'Yahtzee' — comes crashing down to $389,000 because Selina helped cause the very coup that devalued the currency. It's earned on every level: causally, characterologically, and comedically. 'Way to go, ma'am' is the perfect deadpan button. The structure score is very high because this payoff was built all episode. One of the episode's absolute standouts.

jokes.text: All I did was stay focused on one simple goal... to free the peoples of the world from totalitarianism through open and free and transparent elections. And that, in a nutshell, is what I call the Meyer Doctrine.
jokes.setup: Selina is taking a victory lap on the plane after the Caucasian Spring narrative has been credited to her
jokes.punchline: She claims credit for 'freeing the peoples of the world' and names her accidental, corruption-filled involvement 'the Meyer Doctrine' — after spending the entire episode taking bribes and endorsing a fraudulent election
jokes.explanation: The episode's closing ironic statement: Selina, who accepted bribes from both candidates and endorsed a fraudulent election, now claims authorship of Georgian democracy and names it the 'Meyer Doctrine' — directly echoing her mockery of the 'Doyle Doctrine' at the episode's start. The structural circularity is excellent, and this final beat encapsulates Veep's core thesis about political self-delusion.

tmdb_overview: Selina helps monitor the first free and democratic election in Georgia (the country, not the state) and is reunited with an old "friend." Meanwhile, Mike and Gary hide evidence of a major mishap, and Jonah and Richard have an eventful night out. Back in the U.S., Dan tries to help Amy.
insight: Veep's Georgia episode sustains 2.22 jokes per minute through pure character escalation.
